data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-09T01:45:00.225Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about breaking changes when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11, specifically regarding EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider and synchronous LINQ operations.

This is about a future version of .NET (we're in May 2026, so .NET 11 would be in preview). Let me reason about what I know:

In EF Core 9 (with .NET 9), there was a significant change for the Azure Cosmos DB provider: synchronous I/O operations were no longer supported by default. The Cosmos DB provider threw exceptions for sync operations like `ToList()`, `First()`, `Count()`, `SaveChanges()`, etc., because the underlying Cosmos SDK is inherently async.


In EF Core 10, this restriction likely continued, though there may have been a sync-over-async workaround available as an opt-in. For EF Core 11, the trajectory suggests that any remaining sync-over-async compatibility layer would be completely removed, eliminating synchronous operations on the Cosmos DB provider entirely. I need to be careful about what I actually know versus speculation about .NET 11. EF Core 9 made sync operations throw by default with an opt-in available, and the team has signaled that sync-over-async is temporary, so removal is the likely trajectory. The user's code would need to convert operations like `ToList()`, `First()`, `Count()`, and `SaveChanges()` to their async equivalents.
